whatThisIs: Reference the American Society of Anesthesiologists Physical Status Classification - the six-category scale (ASA I through ASA VI, with the E suffix for emergency) that anesthesia teams document for every case. ASA I is a healthy patient, ASA II has mild systemic disease, ASA III has severe systemic disease that is not incapacitating, ASA IV has severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life, ASA V is moribund and not expected to survive without the operation, ASA VI is brain-dead organ donation. Sophie Well lists each class with the ASA-published short examples.
whenToUse: Use this when documenting a pre-operative assessment, when reviewing a chart in which the ASA class drives risk-adjusted outcomes or anesthesia billing modifiers (P1-P6), or when explaining to a patient or trainee how the anesthesia team frames perioperative risk. ASA class is a clinical judgment, not an algorithm - two clinicians can reasonably assign different classes to the same patient. The categories are intentionally broad to be portable across surgical settings; use the published examples as anchors, not the population.
